name: Fermion mass hierarchy
summary: The charged-fermion masses span six orders of magnitude (top ~ 173 GeV down to up ~ 2 MeV; tau ~ 1.78 GeV down to electron ~ 0.51 MeV) with no SM derivation. The Yukawa couplings are O(1) for the top and ~10^-6 for the up quark; this spread looks like a pattern but the SM provides no organising principle.

characteristic_numbers.note: Top Yukawa is the only natural-looking one.

principles_stressed.note: Yukawa couplings have technical naturalness in the Standard Model in the sense that setting y_i to zero restores a chiral symmetry, so small values are not radiatively unstable. The puzzle is 'why are these the values' rather than 'why are they small'; this is closer to a pattern problem than a Higgs-style hierarchy.

history: Recognised as a structural puzzle once the third generation was discovered. Froggatt-Nielsen (1979) proposed an abelian flavor symmetry whose integer charges reproduce the hierarchy via powers of a small expansion parameter. Modular flavor symmetries (Feruglio 2017) and family unification continue to be explored.

references.citation: Froggatt and Nielsen, 'Hierarchy of quark masses, Cabibbo angles and CP violation', Nucl. Phys. B 147, 277 (1979)
Particle Data Group, 'Review of Particle Physics' (2024)
